content.verdict: Perkbox and Ravio both sit in the HR technology space but solve unrelated problems: Perkbox is an employee benefits, discounts, and recognition platform meant to boost day-to-day engagement, while Ravio is a compensation benchmarking and pay-equity platform meant to help People teams set and audit salary structures. Perkbox is built for ongoing employee experience (discounts, wellbeing resources, peer recognition), whereas Ravio is built for periodic, data-heavy compensation decisions backed by live benchmark data from 1,400+ companies. Neither publishes a self-serve price list, but Ravio at least discloses a £5,000/year starting point while Perkbox is fully quote-based.
content.bestForToolA: Perkbox fits HR and people teams that want an always-on perks, discounts, and recognition program — it reports £27.8+ million in employee savings and 106,938+ recognitions sent in a year, and counts Oxford University Hospitals NHS and Bosch as clients.
content.bestForToolB: Ravio fits People and Total Rewards teams at high-growth tech companies that need live, defensible compensation benchmarks and salary bands, especially those already running HiBob, Workday, or Personio, since Ravio syncs with 75+ HR systems.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A scaling tech company could use Ravio to build market-accurate salary bands and run pay equity reviews, then use Perkbox to deliver the discounts, wellbeing support, and recognition programs that round out the total rewards package day to day — the two tools address compensation structure and employee experience separately rather than competing for the same budget line.

keyDifferences.title: Core Function
keyDifferences.toolA: Perkbox centers on employee discounts, wellbeing support, salary sacrifice benefits, and peer-to-peer recognition.
keyDifferences.toolB: Ravio centers on real-time compensation benchmarking, salary bands, pay equity analysis, and compensation review workflows.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Buyers searching for a discounts/perks tool and buyers searching for a pay-benchmarking tool are solving different problems, even though both fall under HR software.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: HR leaders trying to scope which budget category (engagement vs. compensation) a purchase belongs to.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Transparency
keyDifferences.toolA: Perkbox does not publish pricing at all; the site requires filling out an inquiry form for a custom quote.
keyDifferences.toolB: Ravio discloses a starting point: annual plans begin at £5,000/year for the Standard plan.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Even partial pricing transparency helps buyers pre-qualify budget before entering a sales process.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Finance and procurement teams doing early-stage vendor shortlisting.

keyDifferences.title: Data Source and Scale
keyDifferences.toolA: Perkbox's value comes from its discount partner network and reported activation metrics, such as an 89% employee activation rate.
keyDifferences.toolB: Ravio's value comes from live compensation data sourced directly from HR systems of 1,400+ companies across 50+ countries and 300+ roles.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The credibility of each platform rests on different underlying data assets — partner breadth for Perkbox, benchmark breadth for Ravio.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Analysts evaluating whether a platform's core dataset is large enough to be reliable for their use case.

keyDifferences.title: HRIS Integration Depth
keyDifferences.toolA: Perkbox's feature list does not document specific HRIS integrations.
keyDifferences.toolB: Ravio explicitly connects with 75+ HR systems, including HiBob, Workday, and Personio, to sync job, salary, and employee data automatically.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: For a compensation tool, automatic HRIS sync avoids manual data entry errors during review cycles; this is not documented as a Perkbox capability.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: People Ops teams that want compensation data to stay synced with their system of record without manual uploads.

keyDifferences.title: Company Maturity and Backing
keyDifferences.toolA: Perkbox does not disclose its founding year or headquarters on its site.
keyDifferences.toolB: Ravio was founded in 2022, is headquartered in London, and has raised $22M in total funding including a $12M Series A led by Spark Capital.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Funding and founding transparency can factor into vendor risk assessments, especially for a system handling sensitive salary data.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Procurement and security teams performing vendor due diligence before signing a compensation-data contract.

rows.note: Perkbox reports £27.8M+ in annual employee savings
106,938+ recognitions sent per year, per Perkbox

faqs.question: Are Perkbox and Ravio direct competitors?
faqs.answer: No. Perkbox is an employee benefits, discounts, and recognition platform, while Ravio is a compensation benchmarking and pay equity platform. They address different HR budget lines rather than competing for the same purchase.

faqs.question: Which tool publishes pricing?
faqs.answer: Neither publishes a full price list, but Ravio discloses that its annual plans start from £5,000/year. Perkbox requires filling out an inquiry form for any pricing information.

faqs.question: Does Perkbox offer compensation benchmarking?
faqs.answer: No, Perkbox's documented feature set covers discounts, wellbeing, salary sacrifice benefits, recognition, and culture tools — not compensation benchmarking or salary bands.

faqs.question: Does Ravio offer employee discounts or recognition tools?
faqs.answer: No, Ravio's documented features are focused on compensation benchmarking, salary bands, pay equity analysis, and compensation review workflows, not discounts or recognition.

faqs.question: Which HR systems does Ravio integrate with?
faqs.answer: Ravio connects with 75+ HR systems, including HiBob, Workday, and Personio, to automatically sync job, salary, and employee data.

faqs.question: Who are notable customers of each platform?
faqs.answer: Perkbox names Oxford University Hospitals NHS, Bosch, Schuh, Footasylum, and Optiver as clients. Ravio's customer names are not listed in the available data, though it states its data is sourced from 1,400+ companies.

content.verdict: Shopware and Sylius are both open-source ecommerce platforms with free community editions, but they target different buyers: Shopware is a German-built platform with commercial cloud/PaaS tiers, a built-in AI copilot, and GMV-linked enterprise pricing starting at €600/month for Rise, while Sylius is a headless, API-first framework built on Symfony/PHP aimed at developers who want to build fully custom storefronts. Shopware packages more out-of-the-box commerce tooling (CMS, SEO, Flow Builder, B2B components) into paid tiers, while Sylius keeps its commercial layer (Sylius Plus) thin and undisclosed in price, relying instead on developer customization.
content.bestForToolA: Shopware fits mid-market to enterprise retailers who want a vendor-managed AI-assisted commerce platform with built-in CMS, SEO, and B2B tooling and are willing to pay €600/month or more for Rise, Evolve, or Beyond tiers.
content.bestForToolB: Sylius fits development teams comfortable with PHP and Symfony who want a free, MIT-licensed, API-first headless commerce core they can fully customize, backed by a large open-source community of 7,400+ Slack members and 700+ contributors.
content.whoNeedsBoth: There is no realistic scenario where a single storefront would run both platforms together — they are alternative foundations for the same commerce backend, so a team picks one architecture (Shopware's managed suite or Sylius's headless framework) rather than combining them.

keyDifferences.title: Licensing and Base Cost
keyDifferences.toolA: Shopware offers a free, MIT-licensed Community Edition, with paid Rise (from €600/month), Evolve (from €2,400/month), and custom-priced Beyond tiers.
keyDifferences.toolB: Sylius offers a free, MIT-licensed Community Edition, with a commercial Sylius Plus tier whose price is not publicly disclosed.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Shopware's paid tiers have visible starting prices, letting buyers budget in advance, while Sylius Plus requires a sales conversation to learn cost.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Budget-conscious teams that want to compare list prices before engaging sales will find Shopware's Rise/Evolve pricing easier to pre-qualify.

keyDifferences.title: AI Tooling
keyDifferences.toolA: Shopware includes a Shopware Intelligence Copilot for AI-assisted commerce management, with a fuller AI feature set (Shopware Intelligence+) available for an extra €19–€29/month.
keyDifferences.toolB: Sylius's documented feature set does not mention AI-assisted tooling.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Teams wanting built-in AI assistance for merchandising or admin tasks get that natively from Shopware, whereas Sylius would require custom integration.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Merchandising teams that want AI copilot features without building custom tooling.

keyDifferences.title: Architecture Approach
keyDifferences.toolA: Shopware bundles CMS, SEO, analytics, Flow Builder, and Rule Builder into an integrated platform with flexible self-hosted, SaaS, or PaaS deployment.
keyDifferences.toolB: Sylius is built as a headless, API-first framework using REST APIs via API Platform, designed to be extended and customized rather than used as an all-in-one suite.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: An integrated platform reduces implementation time for standard storefronts, while a headless framework gives more control for custom or omnichannel builds.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Retailers wanting fast time-to-launch favor Shopware; agencies building bespoke, API-driven commerce experiences favor Sylius.

keyDifferences.title: B2B Capabilities
keyDifferences.toolA: Shopware offers dedicated B2B components and digital sales rooms available on its higher (Evolve/Beyond) tiers.
keyDifferences.toolB: Sylius supports B2B and B2C commerce natively and offers a dedicated B2B setup product called Elesto, plus a multi-vendor marketplace product called Dafre.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Both platforms address B2B selling, but through different packaging — Shopware gates B2B behind paid tiers, while Sylius offers it as a separate dedicated product.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: B2B-focused merchants evaluating whether B2B tooling is bundled into a subscription (Shopware) or delivered as a distinct implementation (Sylius Elesto).

keyDifferences.title: Ecosystem and Track Record
keyDifferences.toolA: Shopware has a partner ecosystem of 1,200+ certified agencies and was named a Visionary in the 2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for Digital Commerce.
keyDifferences.toolB: Sylius is used by major brands including Mytheresa, Carrefour, and Jägermeister, and has a community of 7,400+ Slack developers and 700+ contributors.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Agency availability (Shopware) versus developer community depth (Sylius) affects how easy it is to staff an implementation team.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprises wanting certified implementation partners lean toward Shopware; engineering-led teams wanting an active open-source contributor base lean toward Sylius.

rows.feature: Published starting price for paid tier
rows.note: Shopware Rise from €600/mo; Sylius Plus is custom/undisclosed
Shopware Intelligence Copilot; add-on €19-29/mo for full suite
Shopware: higher tiers; Sylius: native + Elesto product

faqs.question: Is Shopware or Sylius free?
faqs.answer: Both offer free, MIT-licensed community editions. Shopware's paid tiers (Rise from €600/month, Evolve from €2,400/month, Beyond custom) add cloud hosting and features; Sylius's paid tier, Sylius Plus, has undisclosed pricing.

faqs.question: Does Shopware or Sylius include AI features?
faqs.answer: Shopware includes a Shopware Intelligence Copilot, with an enhanced AI suite available for an extra €19–€29/month. Sylius's documented features do not mention AI tooling.

faqs.question: Which platform is more headless/API-first?
faqs.answer: Sylius is built as an API-first headless framework using REST APIs via API Platform. Shopware also supports headless use cases but is positioned more as an integrated commerce suite with built-in CMS, SEO, and analytics.

faqs.question: Which platform is better for B2B commerce?
faqs.answer: Both support B2B: Shopware gates B2B components and digital sales rooms behind its Evolve and Beyond tiers, while Sylius supports B2B/B2C natively and offers a dedicated B2B product called Elesto.

faqs.question: What technology stack does each platform use?
faqs.answer: Sylius is built on PHP and the Symfony framework with API Platform for its REST APIs. Shopware's underlying stack is not detailed in the available data beyond its self-hosted/SaaS/PaaS deployment options.

faqs.question: Which platform has a larger implementation partner ecosystem?
faqs.answer: Shopware has 1,200+ certified agencies and Gartner Visionary recognition. Sylius has a large open-source community (7,400+ Slack members, 700+ contributors) rather than a formal certified-agency program documented in the data.

content.verdict: Medusa and Sylius are both MIT-licensed, headless, open-source commerce platforms, but they're built on different stacks for different developer audiences: Medusa is a Node.js/TypeScript platform with a managed Medusa Cloud starting at $29/month and built-in Model Context Protocol (MCP) support for AI/agent integrations, while Sylius is a PHP/Symfony platform with a REST API layer via API Platform and an undisclosed-price Sylius Plus commercial tier. The choice mostly comes down to which language ecosystem a team's engineers already work in, plus whether AI/agent tooling is a priority.
content.bestForToolA: Medusa fits JavaScript/TypeScript-first engineering teams who want a modular, MIT-licensed commerce core plus a managed cloud option (from $29/month, priced on infrastructure usage rather than GMV) and native MCP support for AI-agent-driven development.
content.bestForToolB: Sylius fits PHP/Symfony development teams, often agencies, who want a mature, MIT-licensed headless framework with a strong testing culture (BDD) and are building for brands like Mytheresa, Carrefour, or Jägermeister that already run on the Symfony ecosystem.
content.whoNeedsBoth: There's no practical scenario for running both on the same storefront — they are competing backend frameworks in different languages, so the decision is which stack (Node/TypeScript vs. PHP/Symfony) the engineering team standardizes on.

keyDifferences.title: Technology Stack
keyDifferences.toolA: Medusa is built as a modular Node.js/TypeScript commerce platform.
keyDifferences.toolB: Sylius is built on PHP and the Symfony framework, exposing REST APIs via API Platform.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The underlying language determines which engineering talent pool and existing codebase a team can reuse.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Node/TypeScript teams benefit from Medusa; PHP/Symfony teams benefit from Sylius.

keyDifferences.title: Managed Cloud Pricing
keyDifferences.toolA: Medusa Cloud starts at $29/month with no GMV-based fees — pricing scales with infrastructure usage.
keyDifferences.toolB: Sylius Plus (its commercial tier) does not publicly disclose pricing.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A visible starting price lets teams budget a managed option immediately, while an undisclosed price requires a sales conversation.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Startups and small teams wanting quick, transparent cost estimates for a managed deployment.

keyDifferences.title: AI/Agent Tooling
keyDifferences.toolA: Medusa has built-in support for AI/agent integrations via Model Context Protocol (MCP), plus a Cloud CLI and a Development Agent.
keyDifferences.toolB: Sylius's documented feature set does not mention AI or agent-oriented tooling.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Teams building AI-assisted commerce workflows or agentic automation get native support from Medusa without custom integration work.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Engineering teams experimenting with AI agents or LLM-driven development workflows.

keyDifferences.title: GitHub Community Scale
keyDifferences.toolA: Medusa has 35k+ GitHub stars.
keyDifferences.toolB: Sylius's community is described in terms of 7,400+ Slack developers and 700+ contributors rather than star count.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Community size affects how easy it is to find documentation, plugins, and third-party support.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams weighing long-term community support and plugin availability.

keyDifferences.title: Testing and Development Culture
keyDifferences.toolA: Medusa's documented strengths center on modular architecture and multi-regional/multi-currency support rather than a specific testing methodology.
keyDifferences.toolB: Sylius emphasizes a Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) testing approach as a core part of its engineering culture.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A platform with a documented testing philosophy can signal more predictable code quality for teams building mission-critical stores.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprise teams with strict QA requirements may value Sylius's explicit BDD methodology.

faqs.question: Are Medusa and Sylius both free?
faqs.answer: Yes, both have free, MIT-licensed open-source cores. Medusa's paid layer is Medusa Cloud (from $29/month), while Sylius's paid layer, Sylius Plus, has undisclosed pricing.

faqs.question: Which platform supports AI agents natively?
faqs.answer: Medusa supports the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for AI/agent-driven development, along with a Development Agent and Cloud CLI tools. Sylius does not document AI or agent-oriented features.

faqs.question: What programming language is each platform built on?
faqs.answer: Medusa is built on Node.js/TypeScript. Sylius is built on PHP and the Symfony framework, using API Platform for its REST APIs.

faqs.question: How does pricing compare for a managed/cloud deployment?
faqs.answer: Medusa Cloud has a published starting price of $29/month, billed on infrastructure usage with no GMV fees. Sylius Plus pricing is not publicly disclosed and requires contacting sales.

faqs.question: Which platform has a larger open-source community?
faqs.answer: Medusa reports 35k+ GitHub stars. Sylius reports 7,400+ developers on Slack and 700+ contributors — both are large but measured differently in the available data.

faqs.question: Does either platform support B2B or marketplace selling?
faqs.answer: Sylius documents native B2C and B2B support plus a dedicated multi-vendor marketplace product (Dafre) and B2B product (Elesto). Medusa's documented features do not explicitly mention B2B or marketplace-specific tooling.

content.verdict: Medusa and Saleor are both open-source, headless commerce platforms, but they sit at very different price points and maturity levels: Medusa is a Node.js/TypeScript platform with Medusa Cloud starting at $29/month on infrastructure-based pricing, while Saleor is a GraphQL-native platform with Saleor Cloud starting at $1,599/month for its Select tier, plus GMV overage fees. Saleor documents enterprise-grade compliance (SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, PCI-DSS) and scale (1B+ requests, 400k orders processed monthly), while Medusa emphasizes modular architecture and native AI/agent tooling via Model Context Protocol.
content.bestForToolA: Medusa fits teams that want a low-cost entry point into managed headless commerce ($29/month starting) and want built-in AI/agent tooling via MCP for agent-driven development workflows.
content.bestForToolB: Saleor fits larger, compliance-conscious organizations processing significant order volume that need SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, and PCI-DSS compliance out of the box, and are prepared to pay from $1,599/month for the Select Cloud tier.
content.whoNeedsBoth: There's no realistic case for running both together on one store — they're alternative headless commerce backends, so the decision comes down to budget tier and compliance requirements rather than feature complementarity.

keyDifferences.title: Cloud Pricing Tier
keyDifferences.toolA: Medusa Cloud starts at $29/month, priced on infrastructure usage with no GMV-based fees.
keyDifferences.toolB: Saleor Cloud starts at $1,599/month for the Select tier (up to $200,000 GMV, 0.8% overage), rising to $3,999/month for Volume (up to $1,000,000 GMV, 0.4% overage).
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The roughly 55x gap in entry price makes these platforms fit very different budget stages, from early-stage startups to established, high-volume merchants.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Bootstrapped or early-stage teams benefit from Medusa's lower entry cost; large-volume enterprise merchants may accept Saleor's cost for its compliance and scale credentials.

keyDifferences.title: API Architecture
keyDifferences.toolA: Medusa's admin dashboard is customizable and extensible, with modular commerce logic.
keyDifferences.toolB: Saleor is GraphQL-native with 160+ webhooks for integrations and 45+ dashboard mount points for custom UI.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Saleor's GraphQL-first design and extensive webhook count are aimed squarely at complex, high-integration enterprise architectures.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Integration-heavy enterprise teams building many connected systems benefit from Saleor's webhook depth.

keyDifferences.title: Compliance Certifications
keyDifferences.toolA: Medusa's documented pros and cons do not mention formal compliance certifications.
keyDifferences.toolB: Saleor is SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, and PCI-DSS compliant.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Formal compliance certifications are often a hard requirement for enterprise procurement and regulated industries.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprise buyers and regulated industries (finance, healthcare-adjacent retail) with formal vendor security requirements.

keyDifferences.title: Proven Scale
keyDifferences.toolA: Medusa's proof points center on GitHub adoption (35k+ stars) rather than documented transaction volume.
keyDifferences.toolB: Saleor reports handling 1B+ requests and 400k orders processed monthly at scale, with 23.1k+ GitHub stars.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Documented production-scale numbers give buyers more confidence the platform can handle high-traffic, high-order-volume operations.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: High-traffic retailers wanting evidence of proven scale before committing engineering resources.

keyDifferences.title: AI/Agent Tooling
keyDifferences.toolA: Medusa has built-in Model Context Protocol (MCP) support, a Cloud CLI, and a Development Agent for AI/agent-driven development.
keyDifferences.toolB: Saleor's documented features do not mention AI/agent-specific tooling, though it does support agentic sales channels under its omnichannel processing.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Medusa's explicit MCP support is a more direct fit for teams building AI-agent-integrated development workflows today.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams building AI-agent-driven storefront or backend automation.

rows.note: Medusa: self-hosted free; Saleor: Sandboxes tier, non-commercial

faqs.question: How much cheaper is Medusa than Saleor?
faqs.answer: Medusa Cloud starts at $29/month while Saleor Cloud starts at $1,599/month for its Select tier — roughly a 55x difference at the entry point, reflecting their different target markets.

faqs.question: Which platform is more compliance-ready for enterprise?
faqs.answer: Saleor documents SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, and PCI-DSS compliance. Medusa's available data does not list formal compliance certifications.

faqs.question: Does either platform charge based on order volume (GMV)?
faqs.answer: Saleor Cloud charges GMV-based overage fees (0.8% on Select, 0.4% on Volume, as low as 0.2% on Enterprise) beyond each plan's limit. Medusa Cloud explicitly avoids GMV-based fees, pricing on infrastructure usage instead.

faqs.question: Which platform supports AI agents?
faqs.answer: Medusa has built-in Model Context Protocol (MCP) support plus a Cloud CLI and Development Agent for AI/agent-driven development. Saleor's documented features do not specifically mention MCP or agent tooling, though its omnichannel processing supports agentic sales channels.

faqs.question: Are both platforms open source?
faqs.answer: Yes. Medusa's core is MIT-licensed with 35k+ GitHub stars. Saleor's core is BSD-3-Clause licensed with 23.1k+ GitHub stars.

faqs.question: What free options does each platform offer?
faqs.answer: Medusa's self-hosted core is free under MIT. Saleor offers a free Sandboxes tier on Saleor Cloud for non-commercial prototyping and testing, in addition to its free open-source core.

content.verdict: Saleor and Vendure are both open-source headless commerce platforms with paid commercial tiers, but they differ in maturity of their cloud offering and licensing: Saleor Cloud is generally available today starting at $1,599/month, with SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, and PCI-DSS compliance and proven scale (1B+ requests, 400k orders/month), while Vendure's Cloud tier is still in a design-partner stage with general availability targeted for Q4 2026, and its Platform tier requires contacting sales for pricing. Vendure is GPLv3-licensed and built on NestJS/GraphQL with a strong B2B feature set (quote-to-cash, contract pricing), while Saleor is BSD-licensed and GraphQL-native with a heavier enterprise compliance and scale story.
content.bestForToolA: Saleor fits enterprise merchants needing a production-ready, compliance-certified managed cloud today, particularly those with high order volume who can absorb the $1,599/month-plus Select tier cost and its GMV overage fees.
content.bestForToolB: Vendure fits B2B-focused commerce teams that want deep quote-to-cash workflows, contract pricing, and governance tooling (SSO, audit trails, row-level permissions) and are comfortable self-hosting the free GPLv3 Core while Vendure Cloud remains in its design-partner phase.
content.whoNeedsBoth: There's no practical combined use case — both are alternative headless commerce backends, so the decision comes down to whether a team needs Saleor's compliance-certified managed cloud now versus Vendure's deeper native B2B tooling while self-hosting.

keyDifferences.title: License Type
keyDifferences.toolA: Saleor's core is licensed under BSD-3-Clause.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vendure Core is licensed under GPLv3.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: GPLv3 imposes copyleft obligations that BSD does not, which can affect how a company can modify and redistribute the code in commercial products.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Legal and engineering teams evaluating license compatibility with their own commercial software plans.

keyDifferences.title: Cloud Availability
keyDifferences.toolA: Saleor Cloud is generally available now, with published pricing tiers (Select at $1,599/month, Volume at $3,999/month, custom Enterprise).
keyDifferences.toolB: Vendure Cloud is available only to design partners, with general availability targeted for Q4 2026; pricing is not disclosed.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Teams needing a managed hosting option immediately cannot yet fully rely on Vendure Cloud, since it isn't generally available.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams on a near-term launch timeline that need a proven managed cloud option today favor Saleor.

keyDifferences.title: B2B Feature Depth
keyDifferences.toolA: Saleor's documented features focus on omnichannel processing, dynamic product modeling, and a GraphQL-native API rather than named B2B workflows.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vendure documents explicit B2B workflows: quote-to-cash flows, bulk SKU entry, saved requisition lists, repeat-order flows, and customer-specific/contract pricing.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Buyers building B2B wholesale or account-based commerce get more purpose-built tooling out of Vendure's documented feature set.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: B2B and wholesale commerce teams needing quote-to-cash and contract pricing workflows.

keyDifferences.title: Governance and Compliance
keyDifferences.toolA: Saleor documents formal SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, and PCI-DSS compliance.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vendure documents governance tools like SSO, audit trails, row-level permissions, and content versioning, but does not list formal third-party compliance certifications.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Formal compliance certifications (Saleor) versus built-in governance controls (Vendure) serve different procurement checklists.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprise security/compliance teams requiring named certifications lean toward Saleor; teams needing fine-grained internal access control lean toward Vendure.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Transparency
keyDifferences.toolA: Saleor publishes specific tier pricing: Select at $1,599/month and Volume at $3,999/month, with GMV overage fees clearly listed.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vendure's Platform tier is priced as "Custom (contact sales)" with no published figures, and Cloud pricing is undisclosed pending GA.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Published tier pricing lets buyers budget without a sales call, which Vendure's current pricing structure does not support.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Procurement teams that prefer to self-serve pricing information before engaging sales.

rows.feature: SOC 2 Type II / PCI-DSS / GDPR certification

faqs.question: Which platform has a generally available managed cloud today?
faqs.answer: Saleor Cloud is generally available now with published pricing (Select at $1,599/month, Volume at $3,999/month). Vendure Cloud is currently in a design-partner stage, with general availability targeted for Q4 2026.

faqs.question: What license does each platform use?
faqs.answer: Saleor's core is BSD-3-Clause licensed. Vendure Core is GPLv3 licensed, which carries copyleft obligations that BSD does not.

faqs.question: Which platform is better suited to B2B commerce?
faqs.answer: Vendure documents deeper native B2B tooling, including quote-to-cash flows, bulk SKU entry, saved requisition lists, and contract pricing. Saleor's documented features emphasize omnichannel and dynamic product modeling rather than named B2B workflows.

faqs.question: Is Saleor or Vendure free to self-host?
faqs.answer: Both have free, open-source cores that can be self-hosted: Saleor's BSD-licensed core and Vendure's GPLv3-licensed Core, which Vendure describes as free forever.

faqs.question: Which platform has formal compliance certifications?
faqs.answer: Saleor documents SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, and PCI-DSS compliance. Vendure's data does not list formal third-party compliance certifications, though it documents governance features like SSO and audit trails.

faqs.question: Does Vendure's paid tier include a storefront?
faqs.answer: Yes, Vendure Platform includes a production-ready Next.js storefront with company accounts and multi-step checkout, along with 20+ enterprise plugins and AI tooling, on top of Core.

content.verdict: Bagisto and Vendure are both free, open-source commerce platforms but built on different stacks for different use cases: Bagisto is a Laravel/PHP platform geared toward multi-vendor marketplaces, B2B/B2C storefronts, and point-of-sale in one package, while Vendure is a TypeScript/GraphQL (NestJS) headless framework with a deeper, purpose-built B2B feature set like quote-to-cash and contract pricing. Bagisto leans toward breadth (marketplace, POS, omnichannel) out of the box, while Vendure leans toward depth in B2B workflows and governance for complex commerce operations.
content.bestForToolA: Bagisto fits Laravel/PHP development teams that want a free platform to launch a multi-vendor marketplace, B2C storefront, or in-person POS operation from one codebase, backed by a large 26K+ star GitHub community.
content.bestForToolB: Vendure fits TypeScript/Node.js teams building complex B2B commerce with needs like quote-to-cash flows, bulk SKU ordering, and contract pricing, especially those wanting SSO and audit-trail governance controls, as used by brands like IBM, Breitling, and Swile.
content.whoNeedsBoth: There's no realistic scenario for combining them on one storefront — they're alternative commerce backends in different languages (PHP vs. TypeScript), so the choice depends on which stack and B2B depth the engineering team needs.

keyDifferences.title: Technology Stack
keyDifferences.toolA: Bagisto is built on the Laravel PHP framework.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vendure is built on TypeScript, Node.js (NestJS), and GraphQL.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The language ecosystem determines which developers can maintain and extend the platform without ramping up on a new stack.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: PHP/Laravel shops benefit from Bagisto; TypeScript/Node shops benefit from Vendure.

keyDifferences.title: License
keyDifferences.toolA: Bagisto's pricing model is listed as freemium with a free open-source self-hosted core plus paid Bagisto Cloud Hosting (price undisclosed).
keyDifferences.toolB: Vendure Core is GPLv3-licensed and described as free forever, with a separately priced Platform tier requiring contact with sales.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: GPLv3's copyleft terms differ from typical permissive open-source licenses and can affect how derivative commercial products must be licensed.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Legal/engineering teams assessing license compatibility with proprietary extensions they plan to build.

keyDifferences.title: B2B Workflow Depth
keyDifferences.toolA: Bagisto supports B2B and B2C commerce from the same codebase but does not document specific B2B workflow features like quote-to-cash.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vendure documents deep B2B workflows: quote-to-cash flows, bulk SKU entry, saved requisition lists, repeat-order flows, and customer-specific/contract pricing.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Complex B2B buying processes (quotes, approvals, contract pricing) need purpose-built tooling that goes beyond generic B2B/B2C support.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Wholesale and account-based B2B sellers with formal quoting and approval processes.

keyDifferences.title: Marketplace and Omnichannel
keyDifferences.toolA: Bagisto includes built-in multi-vendor marketplace tools, a Point of Sale (POS) module, and multi-tenant SaaS support for hosting multiple stores.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vendure's documented features focus on B2B workflows, pricing, governance, and search rather than a named POS or multi-tenant marketplace module.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Merchants wanting to launch a marketplace with in-person POS in one package have more directly documented support from Bagisto.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Marketplace operators and merchants needing both online and in-person (POS) selling channels.

keyDifferences.title: Governance and Search
keyDifferences.toolA: Bagisto's documented features do not mention SSO, audit trails, or advanced search infrastructure.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vendure documents SSO, audit trails, row-level permissions, content versioning, and Typesense-backed catalog search with a command palette.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Enterprise buyers often require SSO and audit logging as a baseline security requirement; Vendure documents these natively.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprise IT and security teams needing built-in access governance and audit logging.

rows.note: Bagisto Cloud Hosting available now; Vendure Cloud design-partner only

faqs.question: Is Bagisto or Vendure free?
faqs.answer: Both have free, open-source cores. Bagisto's core is free and open source with optional paid Bagisto Cloud Hosting (price undisclosed). Vendure Core is free forever under GPLv3, with a separately priced Platform tier.

faqs.question: Which platform is better for a multi-vendor marketplace?
faqs.answer: Bagisto documents built-in multi-vendor marketplace tools directly in its feature list, along with a POS module. Vendure's documented features focus more on B2B workflows and governance rather than a named marketplace module.

faqs.question: Which platform has deeper B2B features?
faqs.answer: Vendure documents more specific B2B tooling: quote-to-cash flows, bulk SKU entry, saved requisition lists, and contract pricing. Bagisto supports B2B and B2C from the same codebase but doesn't document equivalent named workflows.

faqs.question: What technology stack does each platform use?
faqs.answer: Bagisto is built on Laravel (PHP). Vendure is built on TypeScript, Node.js (NestJS), and GraphQL.

faqs.question: Does either platform offer point-of-sale (POS) functionality?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bagisto includes a documented POS module for in-person selling alongside its online store. Vendure's documented features do not mention POS.

faqs.question: Which platform documents enterprise governance features like SSO?
faqs.answer: Vendure documents SSO, audit trails, row-level permissions, and content versioning as part of its governance tools. Bagisto's documented feature list does not mention these.

content.verdict: Bagisto and OpenCart are both free, self-hosted, open-source ecommerce platforms, but they come from different eras and architectures: Bagisto (founded 2017) is a modern Laravel-based platform with headless API-first architecture, multi-vendor marketplace, B2B support, and POS built in, while OpenCart (founded 2009) is a longer-established PHP platform whose strength is its massive 13,000+ extension marketplace rather than built-in advanced features. Bagisto ships more capability natively; OpenCart relies more heavily on its extension ecosystem to add functionality.
content.bestForToolA: Bagisto fits merchants and Laravel developers who want multi-vendor marketplace, B2B/B2C, POS, and headless API capabilities built into the core platform without needing many third-party extensions, backed by 26K+ GitHub stars.
content.bestForToolB: OpenCart fits store owners who want the longest-running, most battle-tested free PHP cart with the largest extension marketplace (13,000+ listings) to assemble a custom feature set piece by piece.
content.whoNeedsBoth: There's no realistic case for running both together — each is a full, self-contained ecommerce platform, so a merchant picks one as their storefront's core rather than combining them.

keyDifferences.title: Founding Year and Maturity
keyDifferences.toolA: Bagisto was founded in 2017.
keyDifferences.toolB: OpenCart was founded in 2009, giving it roughly eight additional years of ecosystem development.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A longer track record often correlates with a larger extension marketplace and more battle-tested core code, which OpenCart's data supports.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Risk-averse buyers who weight platform longevity heavily may lean toward OpenCart's longer history.

keyDifferences.title: Framework
keyDifferences.toolA: Bagisto is built on the Laravel PHP framework.
keyDifferences.toolB: OpenCart is a standalone PHP shopping cart platform (not documented as built on a specific modern framework like Laravel).
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Laravel's ecosystem (tooling, conventions, package manager) can speed up custom development for teams already familiar with it.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Developers already fluent in Laravel conventions benefit from Bagisto's framework choice.

keyDifferences.title: Extension/Marketplace Ecosystem
keyDifferences.toolA: Bagisto's documented ecosystem centers on GitHub community size (26K+ stars) rather than a large official extension marketplace.
keyDifferences.toolB: OpenCart's official marketplace lists over 13,000 extensions and themes.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A large extension marketplace lets merchants add functionality without custom development, which OpenCart offers at much greater scale.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Non-technical store owners wanting to bolt on features via marketplace purchases rather than custom code benefit from OpenCart.

keyDifferences.title: Built-in Advanced Commerce Features
keyDifferences.toolA: Bagisto natively includes multi-vendor marketplace, B2B and B2C support, headless API-first architecture, POS, omnichannel selling, and multi-tenant SaaS support.
keyDifferences.toolB: OpenCart's core feature set centers on catalog/order management, multi-language/currency, and multiple payment gateways, with advanced features typically added via paid third-party extensions.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Merchants needing marketplace or B2B capability get it natively from Bagisto, while OpenCart users would need to source and pay for extensions to reach feature parity.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Merchants building a marketplace or B2B store from day one benefit from Bagisto's native feature depth.

keyDifferences.title: Managed Hosting Option
keyDifferences.toolA: Bagisto offers an optional Bagisto Cloud Hosting product from Webkul as a managed alternative to self-hosting (pricing not published).
keyDifferences.toolB: OpenCart has no official managed hosting or cloud plan from OpenCart itself.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: An official managed hosting path can simplify operations for merchants who don't want to self-host, an option OpenCart does not provide directly.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Merchants without in-house server administration expertise who still want an officially supported hosting path.

rows.note: Bagisto Cloud Hosting via Webkul; OpenCart has none

faqs.question: Is Bagisto or OpenCart older?
faqs.answer: OpenCart is older, founded in 2009, compared to Bagisto's founding in 2017 — roughly eight years head start.

faqs.question: Which platform has more built-in features?
faqs.answer: Bagisto ships more advanced capability natively, including multi-vendor marketplace, B2B/B2C support, POS, and headless API-first architecture. OpenCart's core is comparatively lean, with advanced functionality typically added through its extension marketplace.

faqs.question: Which has a bigger extension ecosystem?
faqs.answer: OpenCart's official marketplace lists over 13,000 extensions and themes, a scale not documented for Bagisto, which instead emphasizes its 26K+ GitHub star community.

faqs.question: Are Bagisto and OpenCart both free?
faqs.answer: Yes, both have free, open-source, self-hostable cores. Bagisto additionally offers optional paid Bagisto Cloud Hosting; OpenCart has no official managed hosting option.

faqs.question: What frameworks do they use?
faqs.answer: Bagisto is built on the Laravel PHP framework. OpenCart is a standalone PHP application; the available data does not document it being built on a modern framework like Laravel.

faqs.question: Which platform is better suited to a multi-vendor marketplace?
faqs.answer: Bagisto documents built-in multi-vendor marketplace functionality as a core feature. OpenCart's documented feature list does not mention native multi-vendor marketplace support.

content.verdict: OpenCart and Thirty Bees are both free, open-source, self-hosted PHP ecommerce platforms, but they took different paths to get there: OpenCart (founded 2009) grew its own ecosystem into a marketplace of over 13,000 extensions and themes, while Thirty Bees (founded 2017) was created as a fork of PrestaShop 1.6, inheriting compatibility with many PrestaShop 1.6 modules and themes and shipping with 500+ built-in features. OpenCart leans on breadth of third-party extensions; Thirty Bees leans on a larger built-in feature set plus a pre-existing PrestaShop-compatible ecosystem.
content.bestForToolA: OpenCart fits merchants who want the longest-established, most extension-rich free PHP cart (13,000+ marketplace listings) and are comfortable assembling functionality through third-party extensions and themes.
content.bestForToolB: Thirty Bees fits merchants who want a large set of features (500+) already built into the core, or who are already familiar with or invested in the PrestaShop 1.6 module/theme ecosystem and want continued compatibility.
content.whoNeedsBoth: There's no realistic scenario for using both together — both are complete, self-hosted ecommerce platforms, so a merchant chooses one as the foundation of their store rather than running them in parallel.

keyDifferences.title: Origin and Founding Year
keyDifferences.toolA: OpenCart was founded in 2009 as an independent PHP shopping cart project.
keyDifferences.toolB: Thirty Bees was founded in 2017 as a fork of PrestaShop 1.6, created to maintain a stable, compatible alternative.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: OpenCart's longer independent history versus Thirty Bees's inherited PrestaShop lineage shapes each platform's starting feature set and community.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Merchants with existing PrestaShop 1.6 investments benefit from Thirty Bees's compatibility lineage; those wanting an independently evolved platform may prefer OpenCart.

keyDifferences.title: Built-in Feature Count
keyDifferences.toolA: OpenCart's core feature set centers on catalog/order management, multi-language/currency support, and payment gateway integrations, with more advanced functionality typically added via extensions.
keyDifferences.toolB: Thirty Bees ships with 500+ built-in features out of the box, including flexible checkout options, multi-site/multi-currency support, built-in SEO tools, and native PDF invoice generation.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A larger built-in feature count can reduce reliance on paid third-party extensions to reach a usable feature set.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Merchants who want more capability pre-installed without shopping for extensions benefit from Thirty Bees.

keyDifferences.title: Extension/Module Ecosystem Size
keyDifferences.toolA: OpenCart's official marketplace lists over 13,000 extensions and themes.
keyDifferences.toolB: Thirty Bees retains compatibility with many modules and themes built for PrestaShop 1.6, but its own dedicated marketplace and community are described as smaller than platforms like PrestaShop.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A larger, dedicated extension marketplace (OpenCart) generally offers more choice and competition on price for add-on functionality.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Merchants wanting maximum choice in pre-built extensions benefit from OpenCart's larger marketplace.

keyDifferences.title: Active Maintenance Signal
keyDifferences.toolA: OpenCart is described as a long-running project with an active developer community, without a specific recent version date cited.
keyDifferences.toolB: Thirty Bees released version 1.6 in November 2024, cited as evidence of ongoing active development.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A recent release date is a concrete, verifiable signal of active maintenance that buyers can point to.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Buyers wanting reassurance a smaller open-source project is still actively maintained benefit from Thirty Bees's documented recent release.

keyDifferences.title: SEO Tooling
keyDifferences.toolA: OpenCart's documented feature list does not call out dedicated built-in SEO tools.
keyDifferences.toolB: Thirty Bees includes built-in SEO-oriented configuration options as a named feature.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Native SEO configuration reduces the need for separate SEO extensions or manual setup.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Merchants without an SEO extension budget who want baseline SEO controls out of the box.

rows.note: OpenCart: PayPal, Authorize.Net, Amazon Payments, Klarna

faqs.question: Is Thirty Bees related to OpenCart?
faqs.answer: No, they are unrelated projects. Thirty Bees is a fork of PrestaShop 1.6 (created in 2017), while OpenCart is an independently developed PHP shopping cart platform founded in 2009.

faqs.question: Which platform has more built-in features?
faqs.answer: Thirty Bees documents 500+ built-in features out of the box. OpenCart's core is comparatively lean, with advanced functionality typically added via its 13,000+ extension marketplace.

faqs.question: Which has the larger extension marketplace?
faqs.answer: OpenCart's official marketplace lists over 13,000 extensions and themes, a scale not documented for Thirty Bees, though Thirty Bees retains compatibility with many existing PrestaShop 1.6 modules and themes.

faqs.question: Are OpenCart and Thirty Bees both free?
faqs.answer: Yes, both have free, open-source cores that require self-hosting. Thirty Bees notes that some modules, themes, and support are paid add-ons via its marketplace.

faqs.question: Is Thirty Bees still actively maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project released version 1.6 in November 2024, indicating ongoing development. OpenCart's data does not cite a specific recent release date but describes it as a long-running, actively maintained project.

faqs.question: Does either platform include built-in SEO tools?
faqs.answer: Thirty Bees documents built-in SEO-oriented configuration options as a named feature. OpenCart's documented feature list does not call out dedicated SEO tooling.

content.verdict: Spree Commerce and Thirty Bees are both free, open-source ecommerce platforms, but they target very different technical audiences: Spree is a Ruby on Rails, headless, API-first platform with a production-ready Next.js storefront and TypeScript SDK, aimed at developer teams building custom or multi-tenant commerce experiences, while Thirty Bees is a PHP platform forked from PrestaShop 1.6 that ships with 500+ built-in features and a traditional server-rendered storefront, aimed at merchants wanting a more turnkey self-hosted cart. Spree's architecture supports more advanced use cases like multi-tenancy and marketplaces natively documented; Thirty Bees leans on inherited PrestaShop compatibility and built-in breadth.
content.bestForToolA: Spree Commerce fits Ruby on Rails development teams building headless, API-driven storefronts, B2B wholesale portals, or multi-tenant SaaS commerce products, and wanting a ready-made Next.js frontend, as used by brands like Bookshop and Bonobos.
content.bestForToolB: Thirty Bees fits merchants who want a PHP-based, more turnkey self-hosted cart with 500+ built-in features and compatibility with the existing PrestaShop 1.6 module and theme ecosystem, without needing to build a custom headless frontend.
content.whoNeedsBoth: There's no realistic scenario combining them — both are complete, alternative ecommerce platforms built on different languages (Ruby vs. PHP), so a merchant or developer team picks one as their store's foundation.

keyDifferences.title: Technology Stack
keyDifferences.toolA: Spree Commerce's backend is built on Ruby on Rails, with a Next.js storefront and TypeScript SDKs for integration.
keyDifferences.toolB: Thirty Bees is a PHP platform, forked from PrestaShop 1.6, with storefronts built on Bootstrap.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The language and rendering approach (headless API + Next.js vs. traditional server-rendered PHP) determine developer skill requirements and implementation complexity.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Ruby/JavaScript development teams benefit from Spree; PHP-focused teams and merchants wanting simpler server-rendered setup benefit from Thirty Bees.

keyDifferences.title: Architecture Style
keyDifferences.toolA: Spree is headless and API-first, exposing REST APIs and a TypeScript SDK, with the storefront as a separate Next.js application.
keyDifferences.toolB: Thirty Bees uses a traditional, more tightly coupled architecture inherited from PrestaShop 1.6, with Bootstrap-based responsive storefronts.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Headless architecture (Spree) enables more flexible frontend customization and omnichannel delivery, while a coupled architecture (Thirty Bees) can be faster to launch out of the box.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams building custom or omnichannel frontends benefit from Spree's headless design; merchants wanting a faster out-of-the-box launch benefit from Thirty Bees.

keyDifferences.title: Multi-Tenant and Marketplace Support
keyDifferences.toolA: Spree documents multi-tenant architecture for embedding commerce into SaaS products, plus multi-vendor marketplace and B2B wholesale portal support.
keyDifferences.toolB: Thirty Bees documents multi-site and multi-currency support from one installation but does not explicitly document multi-tenant SaaS or marketplace capability.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Multi-tenancy is a specific architectural capability needed for building a platform that serves multiple independent stores from one codebase.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Agencies or SaaS builders wanting to host multiple client stores from a single Spree deployment.

keyDifferences.title: Transaction Fees and Licensing
keyDifferences.toolA: Spree's Community Edition is BSD 3-Clause licensed with no GMV cuts or per-transaction platform fees, per the company.
keyDifferences.toolB: Thirty Bees's core is free and open source, with paid modules, themes, and support available separately via its marketplace.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Confirming there are no revenue-based fees on the free tier matters for merchants projecting long-term platform costs at scale.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: High-volume merchants wanting to avoid GMV-based platform fees benefit from Spree's explicit no-fee stance.

keyDifferences.title: Notable Adopters
keyDifferences.toolA: Spree is used by Bookshop, Bonobos, GoDaddy, and KFC.
keyDifferences.toolB: Thirty Bees's documented data does not list named notable customers.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Named enterprise/consumer-brand adopters can serve as social proof of production readiness at scale.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Buyers wanting evidence of adoption by recognizable brands before committing.

faqs.question: Are Spree Commerce and Thirty Bees both free?
faqs.answer: Yes, both have free, open-source cores. Spree's Community Edition is BSD 3-Clause licensed with no GMV cuts or transaction fees. Thirty Bees's core is free, with paid modules, themes, and support available via its marketplace.

faqs.question: Which platform is headless?
faqs.answer: Spree Commerce is headless and API-first, exposing REST APIs and a TypeScript SDK, with a production-ready Next.js storefront. Thirty Bees uses a more traditional, tightly coupled architecture inherited from PrestaShop 1.6.

faqs.question: What tech stacks do they use?
faqs.answer: Spree's backend is built on Ruby on Rails with a Next.js frontend. Thirty Bees is built on PHP, as a fork of PrestaShop 1.6, with Bootstrap-based storefronts.

faqs.question: Which platform supports multi-tenant or marketplace commerce?
faqs.answer: Spree documents multi-tenant architecture for embedding commerce into SaaS products, plus multi-vendor marketplace support. Thirty Bees documents multi-site/multi-currency support but does not explicitly document multi-tenancy or marketplace capability.

faqs.question: Does Spree charge transaction fees?
faqs.answer: Spree states its Community Edition has no GMV cuts or per-transaction platform fees. Thirty Bees's core is also free, though paid modules, themes, and support are available separately.

faqs.question: Which platform has notable brand customers?
faqs.answer: Spree lists Bookshop, Bonobos, GoDaddy, and KFC as adopters. The available data for Thirty Bees does not list named customers.

content.verdict: Solidus and Spree Commerce share direct lineage — Solidus was forked from Spree in 2015 — and both are free, open-source Ruby on Rails commerce frameworks aimed at developer-led implementations. The key differences that emerged since the fork: Spree emphasizes a headless, API-first architecture with a ready-made Next.js storefront and TypeScript SDK, while Solidus emphasizes deep customization for complex commerce needs, including a native subscriptions engine and both REST and GraphQL APIs. Both keep enterprise pricing (Solidus Cloud, Spree Enterprise Edition) undisclosed and gated behind a sales conversation.
content.bestForToolA: Solidus fits development teams needing highly customized commerce logic with native subscription/recurring-purchase support (curated boxes, recurring models) and are maintained-and-supported by Nebulab and its open-source community.
content.bestForToolB: Spree Commerce fits teams that want a headless commerce backend with a production-ready Next.js storefront already built, reducing frontend implementation time, and want proven adoption at brands like Bookshop, Bonobos, and GoDaddy.
content.whoNeedsBoth: There's no realistic scenario for running both together — as forked siblings built on the same Ruby on Rails foundation, they are direct alternatives, so a team picks one as its commerce framework rather than combining them.

keyDifferences.title: Fork Relationship and History
keyDifferences.toolA: Solidus was forked from Spree in 2015 and has since been maintained independently by Nebulab and the open-source community.
keyDifferences.toolB: Spree Commerce is the original project that Solidus forked from, continuing its own independent development since 2015.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Understanding the fork relationship helps buyers see the two projects as diverging siblings with a shared Rails foundation rather than unrelated platforms.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams researching platform history and long-term project stability before committing engineering resources.

keyDifferences.title: Native Subscriptions Support
keyDifferences.toolA: Solidus includes a native subscriptions engine for curated boxes and recurring purchase models as a documented feature.
keyDifferences.toolB: Spree Commerce's documented feature list does not mention a native subscriptions engine.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Subscription/recurring commerce (e.g., subscription boxes) needs either native support or custom-built logic; Solidus documents this out of the box.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Subscription-box or recurring-order merchants benefit from Solidus's native subscriptions engine.

keyDifferences.title: Storefront Delivery
keyDifferences.toolA: Solidus ships with a starter storefront theme and admin dashboard, without a specifically named modern JS framework for the frontend.
keyDifferences.toolB: Spree ships with a production-ready Next.js storefront that connects directly to the Rails backend, plus a TypeScript SDK.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A pre-built, production-ready modern frontend (Spree's Next.js storefront) can meaningfully cut implementation time versus building on a starter theme.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams wanting to launch a modern JS-based storefront quickly benefit from Spree's included Next.js option.

keyDifferences.title: API Options
keyDifferences.toolA: Solidus provides both REST and GraphQL APIs for building custom frontends and integrations.
keyDifferences.toolB: Spree exposes REST APIs and a TypeScript SDK; GraphQL support is not documented in the available data.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Offering both REST and GraphQL (Solidus) gives integration teams more flexibility in how they query and mutate commerce data.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Integration engineers who prefer GraphQL for complex data-fetching needs benefit from Solidus's dual API support.

keyDifferences.title: Enterprise/Cloud Tier
keyDifferences.toolA: Solidus Cloud (Enterprise) is offered as a fully hosted option with 24/7 support and unlimited usage, but pricing is not publicly listed.
keyDifferences.toolB: Spree's Enterprise Edition offers dedicated support and mission-critical deployment assistance, also with undisclosed pricing.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Both platforms gate their highest support tier behind a sales conversation, so neither offers self-serve enterprise pricing.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprise buyers should expect a custom sales process either way, regardless of which sibling platform they choose.

faqs.question: Is Solidus related to Spree Commerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Solidus was directly forked from Spree in 2015 and has been maintained independently since then by Nebulab and the open-source community.

faqs.question: Which platform has native subscription support?
faqs.answer: Solidus documents a native subscriptions engine for curated boxes and recurring purchase models. Spree Commerce's documented features do not mention native subscription support.

faqs.question: Which platform ships with a ready-made modern storefront?
faqs.answer: Spree Commerce ships with a production-ready Next.js storefront and TypeScript SDK. Solidus ships with a starter theme and admin dashboard without a specifically named modern JS frontend.

faqs.question: Do both platforms support GraphQL?
faqs.answer: Solidus documents both REST and GraphQL APIs. Spree's documented API is REST-based with a TypeScript SDK; GraphQL support is not documented for Spree.

faqs.question: Are Solidus and Spree Commerce both free?
faqs.answer: Yes, both are free, open-source frameworks that can be self-hosted. Each also offers an undisclosed-price hosted enterprise tier: Solidus Cloud (Enterprise) and Spree's Enterprise Edition.

faqs.question: Who maintains each project?
faqs.answer: Solidus is maintained by Nebulab and the open-source community. Spree Commerce's maintaining organization is not specified in the available data beyond its open-source community and BSD 3-Clause licensing.
